If you encounter `error_2` when trying to **Install permanent SSH**, it may be caused by a mismatch between the kernel version and the xmir patcher's kernel module (`5.4.0+` vs `5.4.55`).

You can verify this by running:
(At this stage, you should already have telnet access, or even temporary SSH access if you have r

> If you encounter `error_2` when trying to **Install permanent SSH**, it may be caused by a mismatch between the kernel version and the xmir patcher's kernel module (`5.4.0+` vs `5.4.55`).

This is very strange kernel behavior when loading a `kmod` (via `insmod`). 
This shouldn't happen, since the kernel 5.4.x is compiled with these options:
```
CONFIG_MODULES=y
# CONFIG_MODULE_FORCE_LOAD is not

> what error does utility insmod return?

There wasn't any useful info from `insmod` other than a general error message.

But `dmesg | grep xmir_patcher.ko` indeed gave the following information, which leaded me to that trick.

```shOut
xmir_patcher.ko: version magic '5.4.0+ SMP mod_unload ARMv7 p2v8' should be '5.4.55 SMP mod_unload ARMv7 p2v8'
```

And I tried to execute the following scripts manually from telnet, and it indeed started SSH session:
```sh
/etc/crontabs/dropbearmulti/install.sh

```

and I didn't even know about it

1. create a file `/etc/crontabs/dropbearmulti/maybe_install.sh` with the following content (copied from `install.sh` with most content removed)

  ```sh
  #!/bin/sh

  XDIR=/etc/crontabs/dropbearmulti

  if [ ! -d /etc/dropbear ]; then
    $XDIR/install.sh
  fi
  ```

2. make it executable by

  ```sh
  chmod a+x /etc/crontabs/dropbearmu

open_issues_sample_with_comments.body_preview:
Hi everybody,

I've realized that last browser versions of the main browsers (IE, Firefox, Chrome, Safari) avoid evercookie.

Is there any way to make evercookie work even if user uses private mode?

Thanks,

FMN

comments.body_preview:
@fidelmartin: By design, Private Browsing Mode is intended to defeat things like evercookie. If evercookie circumvents private browsing mode, browser developers treat it as a bug and adjust accordingly.

In fact, that's by design too. Evercookie wasn't the first thing to do what it does, but it was the first one that was announced publicly so browser manufacturers would improve their privacy contr

@fidelmartin: Sorry. I understand your point of view, but all my experience is more in using things like NoScript and careful tuning of my browser headers to limit Panopticlick's utility as much as possible. (Also, as I remember, the Firefox guys adjusted their user agent strings about a year ago so their Gecko build dates would be less variable, providing less uniqueness for Panopticlick to latch

A possible fix I found is to user browser fingerprint in conjunction with evercookie.

_1) You open Chrome and you get a evercookie saved (let's say with value AAA).
2) And then open Chrome in Incognito mode and get a new value for the evercookie (let's say BBB).
And you have the problem._

But... The browser fingerprint is the same for both Incognito and non-Incognito...
So my fix is to store ser

Looks like more incentive for browser manufacturers to simplify their headers further.

Firefox is already making decent progress on that:
1. They've simplified their User-Agent string in recent versions, as has Internet Explorer.
2. The most reliable way to fingerprint a machine is to use plugins like Flash, Java, and Silverlight to grab things like the Ethernet MAC address or the list of install

I use a flash to get list of installed fonts. However doesn't work in iPads/ipods/MAC.

I agree that the best is to get MAC address but with Flash and Silverlight is not possible. With Java applets I think is possible but I think the browser advice user that an applet want to access system. If you know a way to get MAC address without advicing user I would appreciate it.
@fidelmartin As far as I know, it's not possible. (If it were, I'd be conflicted about telling people before Firefox gains its built-in plugin whitelisting to help protect against it)
Is there any possibility to detect private mode?
No reason to store anything. And no reason to count that user in Vote rating data, for example.

@zxqfox **Bad idea.** There are a lot of people who (like one of my friends) run with Private Browsing on permanently or (like me) use it as a simple, intuitive way to visit a site they don't trust (eg. one that integrates with Google or Facebook for login) and then flush all data that could be used to track them.

In other words, there are a lot of people who use Private Browsing and/or Incognito
There is no anonymous really. Google knows who you are even in private mode. https://panopticlick.eff.org/ Even in private or any mode.

I have implemented Panopticlick idea but just considering only the information that almost not change, system fonts and browser plugins. I think the results are good enoug.

Hi,

comments.body_preview:
@gdecostanzo 

My rough guess on this:

This [line](https://github.com/samyk/poisontap/blob/master/pi_startup.sh#L52) redirects traffic destinated to TCP port 80 of some foreign IP to **127.0.0.1**:1337. Where setting 127.0.0.1 is implied by the REDIRECT statement (see iptables manpage for details). So basically this DNAT to localhost.

What you need instead is MASQUERADING on your outbound
@mame82 though my post is a bit late, here is how you do it: http://raspi.tv/2015/ethernet-on-pi-zero-how-to-put-an-ethernet-port-on-your-pi

@MarnixBouhuis the for pointing out. Interesting read, diving into SPI is on my ToDo list. Anyway, switched to Pi Zero W in meantime, so Bluetooth and WiFi is available.
Could ETH over SPI reach 1 GBit/s ?

@mame82 those spi network interfaces are most of the time capable of doing 10 Mbps. But the spi bus speed is a bottleneck, even if you increase the bus speed (spi-max-frequency = 20000000) you will get speeds around 400-500 Kbps. This is based on the Ethernet module I used (ENC28J60). You should always look at the frequency supported by the mic and pi when increasing the spi bus speed.

You could use a USB hub on the OTG port to connect to a USB-to-Ethernet dongle and the target machine. The Pi's USB bus itself theoretically maxes out at 480Mbps, so a network card could get some decent speed, though not 1Gbps.
